name: Insect Bites & Stings
ontario_ma_scope: Insect bites/stings are NOT a designated Ontario MA condition. Pharmacist role is OTC counselling and referral. Cross-reference: Tick Bite/Lyme is a separate Ontario MA condition.

key_questions: Type of insect (bee/wasp sting vs mosquito/fly bite)?
History of previous severe reactions to the same insect?
Stinger still present (bee stings — remove immediately)?
Lyme disease risk (tick bite — see Tick Bite protocol)?
red_flags: Systemic symptoms (hives beyond bite site, wheezing, hypotension, dizziness, throat tightness) → ANAPHYLAXIS — CALL 911, epinephrine
Large local reaction expanding rapidly over hours → cellulitis vs severe local reaction
Fever + expanding warmth/redness at bite site → secondary infection
Multiple bee/wasp stings → systemic toxicity risk
If anaphylaxis symptoms — prescribe/recommend EpiPen auto-injector, teach technique, advise carry 2, call 911 even after epinephrine use

first_line.name: Local Reaction Management
first_line.drugs: Hydrocortisone 1% cream TID-QID × 3–5 days (itch/swelling) | Cetirizine (Reactine) 10 mg OD (systemic itch/urticaria) | Acetaminophen 500–1000 mg or ibuprofen 400 mg q6–8h for pain from stings
first_line.notes: Remove stinger (bee): Remove by any means quickly — speed of removal matters more than method. Scraping with a card or nail is acceptable; so is grasping with fingers. Avoid squeezing the venom sac. Ice 10–15 min immediately — reduces swelling and pain. Topical hydrocortisone + oral antihistamine for local reaction. ⚠️ Topical diphenhydramine is generally discouraged — risk of contact sensitization (RxFiles, NICE CKS). Use topical hydrocortisone 1% instead for local itch.
first_line.preferred_when: Local reaction only (redness/swelling confined to bite area)

second_line.name: Short-Course Oral Corticosteroid
second_line.drugs: Prednisone 40–60 mg × 3–5 days; no taper needed for courses <7 days
second_line.notes: For large local reactions (>10 cm) or prominent systemic hives (urticaria beyond bite site) that do not respond to antihistamines.

non_pharm: Remove stinger (bee) by any means quickly — speed matters more than method; scraping with card or nail is fine; avoid squeezing venom sac
Wash bite area with soap and water
Ice pack 10–15 min × q1–2h for first 24h
Calamine lotion: soothing for mild itch
Prevention: insect repellent (DEET 20–30%), cover skin in wooded areas, avoid floral perfumes, food odours when outdoors
refer_when: Anaphylaxis — prescribe/recommend EpiPen auto-injector, teach technique, advise carry 2, call 911 even after epinephrine use; refer to allergist
Epinephrine auto-injector prescription + allergist referral for known severe reactors
Large local reactions (>10 cm) not responding to antihistamines/steroids
Secondary infection (expanding erythema, warmth, fever, purulent discharge)
Tick bite with >36h attachment (Lyme prophylaxis protocol)
Mosquito bite in traveller returning from tropical region (malaria, dengue, Zika, EEE/WNV risk)
Multiple stings (>10 in children, >50 in adults — toxicity risk)
Unusual or worsening reaction not responding to OTC management

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Insect Bites and Stings. OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
RxTx (Canadian Pharmacists Association). Insect Bites and Stings — Therapeutic Choices, 8th ed. (2023).
AAD (American Academy of Dermatology). Insect Bite Reactions: Diagnosis and Management (2022).
WHO. Vector Control: Insect Repellents and Bite Prevention. World Health Organization (2023).
